translation: Ya, Seen
commentary:
**يسٓ**

Ya-Sin. These are the opening letters of this remarkable surah, and scholars have offered various interpretations of their meaning. One compelling view suggests that Ya-Sin may be a divine address to the Prophet ﷺ himself. The letter "Ya" functions as a call of attention, while "Sin" represents the central letter of the Arabic word "insan", meaning human being. According to this interpretation, these letters together address the Prophet ﷺ as the essence of humanity, the perfect example of what it means to be human. He ﷺ is the central figure around which all of humanity revolves.

While we cannot claim this interpretation with absolute certainty, it reflects the profound connection between these mysterious letters and the themes that follow. What we can say with confidence is that Ya-Sin belongs to the category of disconnected letters (Huruf Muqatta'at) that appear at the beginning of certain surahs, inviting us to contemplate the miraculous nature of the Quran itself - how these simple letters, when divinely arranged, produce guidance that transforms hearts and nations.

translation: By the wise Qur'an
commentary:
**وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ**

By the Quran, full of wisdom. Here Allah ﷻ takes an oath, and this marks our entry into a section of the Quran where oaths become increasingly prominent. Understanding the purpose and power of these divine oaths is essential to appreciating what follows.

In Arabic literary tradition, oaths served multiple sophisticated functions beyond what we typically understand. Yes, oaths are used in moments of anger, in court proceedings, or when someone doubts your truthfulness. But in classical Arabic, oaths had two additional critical purposes that elevated them to an art form.

First, oaths captured attention. When a speaker began with an oath, everyone knew something urgent was about to be announced. The audience would stop and listen intently. But more than that, the object of the oath - the thing being sworn by - would hint at the nature and importance of what was coming. If someone swore by the morning, people understood that the announcement concerned something happening at dawn.

Second, and this is where the Quran elevated the oath to divine eloquence, the object of the oath serves as evidence for the claim that follows. The Quran presents the proof before making the statement. The object becomes a witness testifying to the truth of the subject. When Allah ﷻ says "by time, indeed mankind is in loss" (103:1-2), time itself stands as a witness. All of human history testifies to humanity's state of loss. Every moment that passes, every breath we take bringing us closer to death, serves as evidence of our need for divine guidance.

So when Allah ﷻ swears by the Quran full of wisdom (القرآن الحكيم), He ﷻ is presenting the Quran itself as the primary evidence for what comes next. The wisdom contained in this Book - wisdom that no human being could produce independently - serves as testimony to the truth about to be declared.

The description "الحكيم" means full of wisdom, but it also carries the meaning of something that judges with precision, that puts everything in its proper place. The Quran is not just wise; it is wisdom itself, perfectly balanced and flawlessly structured.

translation: Indeed you, [O Muhammad], are from among the messengers
commentary:
**إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ**

Indeed, you are truly among the messengers. This is the subject of the oath - the claim for which the Quran serves as evidence. Notice the emphatic construction: إِنَّكَ meaning "indeed you," followed by لَ, another particle of emphasis. The Arabic is stacked with affirmation: without doubt, certainly, absolutely, you are among those who have been sent.

This direct address to the Prophet ﷺ comes with the full weight of divine testimony. The Quran itself stands as witness to his prophethood. He ﷺ doesn't need validation from human beings. He ﷺ is not dependent on people's acceptance or rejection. The greatest testifier to his message is the Book itself - its wisdom, its eloquence, its guidance, its transformation of hearts and societies.

Think about this carefully: the object of the oath is the Quran. The Quran testifies that you are a messenger. How does the Quran testify? By its very nature. The wisdom it contains is impossible for a human being to produce. The literary excellence, the profound insights into human nature, the accurate predictions, the timeless guidance - all of this serves as evidence that this Book could only come from Allah ﷻ, and therefore the one who brought it must truly be His Messenger ﷺ.

The phrase "مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ" - "among the messengers" - also places the Prophet ﷺ in the noble company of all the prophets who came before. He ﷺ is not an innovation or an oddity in human history. He ﷺ continues the tradition of Ibrahim (AS), Musa (AS), Isa (AS) and all the prophets who received revelation and called their people to truth.

translation: On a straight path
commentary:
**عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ**

Upon a straight path. This is not merely describing the message; it's describing the character and conduct of the Messenger ﷺ himself. You are not just delivering a message about the straight path - you embody it. Your character, your conduct, your every action demonstrates what it means to walk the Sirat al-Mustaqim.

The word "عَلَىٰ" means "upon" or "firmly established on." There's stability here, confidence, unwavering commitment. The Prophet ﷺ is not wobbling or wavering. He ﷺ stands firm on this straight path regardless of opposition, persecution, or temptation.

This ayah connects directly to what preceded it. The Quran is full of wisdom, and you are truly a messenger, and this is evidenced by the fact that you are upon a straight path. Your character itself serves as proof of your message. If someone claimed prophethood but lived a crooked life, who would believe them? But the Prophet ﷺ lived with such impeccable character that even his enemies called him "Al-Amin" - the trustworthy one.

The straight path is also an invitation. By describing the Prophet ﷺ as being upon this path, Allah ﷻ shows us what it looks like to walk it. We're not left to wonder what the straight path means in practice - we can look at his life ﷺ and see it manifested in human form.

translation: [This is] a revelation of the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
**تَنزِيلَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ**

A revelation from the All-Mighty, the Most Merciful. Now we learn the source and nature of what has been given to the Prophet ﷺ. The word "تَنزِيلَ" is critical here. It doesn't just mean "revelation" in a general sense. The form of the word implies gradual descent, revelation that comes down little by little, piece by piece, over time.

This wasn't a book dropped all at once. The Quran descended gradually over twenty-three years, addressing situations as they arose, guiding the community through challenges step by step. This gradual revelation served multiple purposes: it allowed the companions to absorb and implement the guidance, it responded to events as they unfolded, and it demonstrated the living, dynamic nature of divine communication.

The revelation comes from "ٱلْعَزِيزِ" - the All-Mighty, the One who cannot be overcome, the Ultimate Authority. There is power behind this revelation. It's not the word of a weak deity who can be challenged or ignored. It comes with the full authority of the One who controls all of existence.

Yet immediately we're told it's also from "ٱلرَّحِيمِ" - the Most Merciful, the One whose mercy is continuous and specific to believers. This combination is profound. The same God who possesses ultimate power and authority is also infinitely merciful. The revelation that could crush you with its demands instead lifts you with its mercy. The law that could condemn you instead guides you to salvation.

These two names together tell us something essential about how to understand the Quran. Yes, there are warnings and consequences. Yes, there are commands and prohibitions. But all of it comes from a place of mercy, from a God who wants good for His creation, who reveals guidance not to burden but to liberate.

translation: That you may warn a people whose forefathers were not warned, so they are unaware
commentary:
**لِتُنذِرَ قَوْمًا مَّآ أُنذِرَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ فَهُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ**

So that you may warn a people whose forefathers were not warned, and so they are heedless. Here we learn the purpose of this revelation and the mission of the Prophet ﷺ: to warn. The word "لِتُنذِرَ" means to give warning, to alert someone to danger, to wake them up to something serious approaching.

The people being addressed are the Arabs, specifically the Quraysh. Their forefathers were not warned - no prophet had been sent to them in recent memory. The last messenger in that region was Ismail (AS), thousands of years earlier. Since then, no warner had come specifically to the Arab people in their language.

The consequence of this gap in prophetic guidance is described powerfully: "فَهُمْ غَـٰفِلُونَ" - they are heedless, completely oblivious, utterly careless. The word "ghafiloon" describes someone who is so relaxed and carefree that they've forgotten there's anything to worry about. They've become comfortable in their ignorance, settled into their way of life without questioning whether there might be consequences.

Imagine someone living in a house that's slowly filling with gas. They can't smell it, they can't see it, and because they've been in there so long, they don't even think about danger. That's the state of those who haven't received warning for generations. They inherit beliefs and practices without questioning them. They live life thinking this is all there is - no accountability, no judgment, no higher purpose.

This ayah explains why the message was needed so urgently in Makkah. These weren't people who had rejected clear guidance. They were people who had never received it. They and their ancestors had lived in a state of heedlessness, and now the Prophet ﷺ was being sent to shake them awake.

translation: Already the word has come into effect upon most of them, so they do not believe
commentary:
**لَقَدْ حَقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَىٰٓ أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ**

The word has already proven true against most of them, and they will not believe. This is a difficult ayah, one that confronts us with a painful reality. Despite the clear evidence - the wise Quran, the impeccable character of the Prophet ﷺ, the undeniable signs - most of them will not believe.

The phrase "حَقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ" means the word, the verdict, the decree has become binding and deserved. Allah's ﷻ statement about them has proven true through their own behavior. They have shown through their actions and attitudes that they are among those who will reject guidance no matter how clear it is.

This isn't predestination in the sense that Allah ﷻ forced them into disbelief. Rather, it's divine knowledge of their character and choices. Allah ﷻ knows who will persist in arrogance and who will humble themselves before truth. Those who have hardened their hearts, who have chosen pride over humility, have essentially rendered themselves incapable of accepting guidance.

The phrase "فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ" - "they will not believe" - is stated as a fact. As far as Allah's ﷻ knowledge is concerned, these are terminal cases. They've exhibited such stubborn resistance that there's no hope for their guidance.

This reality was important for the Prophet ﷺ to understand. He ﷺ would exhaust himself trying to guide people, sometimes grieving over their rejection. But Allah ﷻ reminds him: some people have already sealed their own fate through their choices. You can't force guidance into a heart that has locked itself against truth.

translation: Indeed, We have put shackles on their necks, and they are to their chins, so they are with heads [kept] aloft
commentary:
**إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا فِىٓ أَعْنَـٰقِهِمْ أَغْلَـٰلًا فَهِىَ إِلَى ٱلْأَذْقَانِ فَهُم مُّقْمَحُونَ**

Indeed, We have placed on their necks chains up to their chins, so their heads are held up. Now we encounter one of the most vivid and striking images in the entire Quran. Allah ﷻ describes the spiritual state of these obstinate disbelievers through a powerful physical metaphor.

"أَغْلَـٰلًا" refers to shackles or chains, like the iron collars that were placed on prisoners and slaves. These restraints go around the neck, but in this case, they extend "إِلَى ٱلْأَذْقَانِ" - up to the chins. The word "adhqan" refers to the area of the chin and lower jaw, particularly someone with a full beard.

Picture this: a collar is placed around someone's neck, but instead of staying at neck level, it forces the head upward until the collar reaches the chin. What happens? The person's face is pushed up, their head tilted back. Try this yourself - tilt your head back and try to look at something right in front of your feet. You can't see it, no matter how hard you try.

"فَهُم مُّقْمَحُونَ" - they are muqmah. This word comes from a description of a camel that raises its head but keeps its eyes looking downward. It's an unnatural, strained position. The camel wants to look down but its head is held up.

The symbolism is multilayered and profound. First, holding your head high in this forced way represents arrogance. Throughout the Quran, Allah ﷻ condemns those who walk with their heads held high in pride. The people of Makkah showed such arrogance toward the Prophet ﷺ - looking down on him despite his impeccable character, dismissing his message despite its wisdom.

But here's the consequence: their arrogance has now locked them in that position. They wanted to hold their heads high in rejection? Fine. Now they're stuck that way, unable to look down and see the truth that's right in front of them. The revelation is directly in their path, clear as daylight, but they can't see it because their heads are locked in a position of arrogance.

This is a spiritual reality made visible. When you persist in pride, when you refuse to humble yourself before truth, you eventually lose the ability to see truth at all. Your arrogance becomes your prison. The very attitude you chose as a display of superiority becomes the chain that keeps you bound in ignorance.

translation: And We have put before them a barrier and behind them a barrier and covered them, so they do not see
commentary:
**وَجَعَلْنَا مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ سَدًّا وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ سَدًّا فَأَغْشَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ**

And We have placed before them a barrier and behind them a barrier, and We have covered them, so they do not see. The imagery continues and intensifies. Not only are their heads locked in an upward position, but now barriers have been erected all around them.

"سَدًّا" is a barrier or wall, something insurmountable that blocks passage. There's a wall "مِنۢ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ" - in front of them, between their hands, right in their forward path. And another wall "مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ" - behind them. They're trapped between two walls with no escape route.

But it gets more suffocating: "فَأَغْشَيْنَـٰهُمْ" - We have covered them, veiled them, thrown a covering over them. Even if they could turn their heads, even if they could look to the side, they still wouldn't see because they're covered in darkness.

"فَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ" - they do not see, they cannot see. Complete blindness. Complete obstruction. No way forward, no way back, no vision in any direction.

This is the ultimate description of spiritual blindness. These people are surrounded by evidence of Allah's ﷻ existence and the truth of His Messenger ﷺ. The signs are everywhere - in creation, in history, in the Quran itself, in the character of the Prophet ﷺ. But they cannot see any of it. Why? Because their own choices have erected these barriers.

The two walls mentioned - one in front and one behind - can be understood as representing two types of evidence that the Quran repeatedly presents. The wall in front represents the signs of creation all around us, the evidence in the natural world, the miracles we witness daily but take for granted. The wall behind represents the lessons of history, the stories of nations that came before, the patterns that repeat across time.

Both types of evidence are mentioned throughout the Quran. "Do they not look at the camels, how they are created? At the sky, how it is raised?" - these are the signs in front of them. And "Has there not come to you the stories of those before?" - these are the signs behind them, in history.

But despite being surrounded by evidence, despite the truth being right in front of them and lessons of history behind them, they see nothing. This is the terrifying endpoint of persistent rejection. You don't just remain neutral - you actively become unable to perceive truth. Your rejection builds walls around you until you're trapped in a prison of your own making, unable to see the very guidance that could set you free.

These opening ayahs establish several critical themes that will echo throughout the surah: the undeniable prophethood of Muhammad ﷺ, the Quran as evidence of divine origin, the tragic reality that some people will reject clear guidance, and the spiritual consequences of persistent arrogance and heedlessness. The imagery is vivid, the message is urgent, and the stakes couldn't be higher.

translation: And it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them - they will not believe
commentary:
وَسَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

*And it is the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them—they will not believe.*

Allah ﷻ has already indicated that there are people who represent a lost cause, and now He fortifies that reality. The verdict is sealed: whether you warn them or don't warn them, it makes no difference. They will not believe. This is not a statement made at the beginning of the Prophet's mission ﷺ, but after extensive da'wah, after every avenue has been exhausted, after hearts have been tested and found permanently closed.

translation: You can only warn one who follows the message and fears the Most Merciful unseen. So give him good tidings of forgiveness and noble reward
commentary:
إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَخَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِمَغْفِرَةٍ وَأَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ

*You can only warn one who follows the reminder and fears the Most Merciful in the unseen. So give him glad tidings of forgiveness and noble reward.*

إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ—"You only warn..." The particle إِنَّمَا restricts and limits: your warning will only be effective for a specific type of person. مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلذِّكْرَ—"whoever followed the reminder."

To follow something suggests that something in you resonates with it. Imagine walking across campus and catching the aroma of food cooking. Something familiar registers, and you follow your nose toward the source. Similarly, the only people you can truly warn are those who, when they hear the reminder, find something inside themselves that clicks with it. They feel an inclination toward it. They follow because something within them recognizes the truth.

وَخَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ—"and was fearful of the Most Merciful in the unseen." This connects directly to the previous ayahs where we explored the manifestations of Allah's رَحْمَة. Those manifestations should actually inspire a sense of purpose-driven fear. If everything else in creation has a purpose, then I—more than any of these things—must have a purpose.

The bee doesn't look at the sun and marvel at its magnificence; it simply does its job. The flower doesn't observe the mountain and say, "How incredible!" Other creatures fulfill their roles without self-awareness. We are the only creation of Allah ﷻ that looks around and appreciates all the other signs of Allah ﷻ. We can even observe and appreciate our own creation. As Allah ﷻ says: فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ (Let the human being observe from what he was created). We can examine ourselves—how we're designed, how we function. This is extraordinary.

If everything else has a clear purpose, we should be even more accountable for our purpose than anything else. We are the most purposeful of all creation, and that's a weighty, even frightening thought.

The phrase بِٱلْغَيْبِ carries dual meanings. First, it means fearing Rahman even though He is in the unseen—we cannot see Him, yet we remain conscious of Him. But it also means fearing Rahman when we ourselves are in the unseen—when nobody else is watching, in our private moments. When we're hidden from people, we're still aware that we're not hidden from Him.

فَبَشِّرْهُ بِمَغْفِرَةٍ وَأَجْرٍ كَرِيمٍ—"So give him glad tidings of forgiveness and noble reward." Notice the singular pronoun. After describing the general characteristics, Allah ﷻ switches to addressing each individual directly. Give *him* the good news. This personalizes the message—every person who follows the reminder and fears Allah ﷻ in private deserves to hear individually that forgiveness and generous reward await them.

translation: Indeed, it is We who bring the dead to life and record what they have put forth and what they left behind, and all things We have enumerated in a clear register
commentary:
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا۟ وَءَاثَـٰرَهُمْ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ فِىٓ إِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ

*Indeed, it is We who bring the dead to life, and We record what they have sent ahead and what they left behind. And We have enumerated all things in a clear register.*

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ—"Indeed, We are the ones who give life to the dead." The emphatic structure—إِنَّا (indeed) and نَحْنُ (We)—stresses Allah's exclusive power over life and death. This is the resurrection, the bringing back to life of all who have died.

وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا۟—"And We record what they have sent ahead." Every action has consequences. We learned previously that every deed returns to you—you send something out, it comes back. Now we learn that Allah ﷻ documents everything. Every action is written down by the angels and sent ahead for processing to the Department of Divine Justice, if you will. Everything is documented.

وَءَاثَـٰرَهُمْ—"and their traces." The word أَثَر literally means footsteps in sand, remnants, traces of something that once existed. Allah ﷻ is saying that your actions have consequences far beyond your lifetime. Just as you see footprints and know someone walked there, or you smell smoke and know a car with bad transmission drove by, your deeds leave lasting traces.

These aren't just your direct actions—these are the ripple effects. You did something wrong, and as a result your children did something wrong, and their children continued in that wrong. These are the آثَار of your deeds, the remnants and consequences that extend through time. Similarly, the good you do continues to benefit others long after you're gone. This is what makes sadaqah jariyah so powerful—it's the positive athar that keeps giving.

وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ أَحْصَيْنَـٰهُ—"And everything We have enumerated." The verb أَحْصَىٰ means to count something completely, to encompass it, to have a full and comprehensive record. Not a single thing escapes Allah's accounting.

فِىٓ إِمَامٍ مُّبِينٍ—"in a clear imam." The word إِمَام has multiple meanings in Arabic. It means a leader whom people follow, but it also means a road or path. The image here is of a book so vast and comprehensive that it resembles a highway—you keep scrolling and it keeps unfolding, going on and on. That's how massive the document is that records absolutely everything. مُّبِينٍ means clear, manifest, open—there's no ambiguity, no hidden clauses, no fine print. Everything is documented with perfect clarity.

translation: And present to them an example: the people of the city, when the messengers came to it
commentary:
وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ إِذْ جَآءَهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ

*And present to them an example: the people of the town, when the messengers came to it.*

وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا—"And strike for them an example." The verb ضَرَبَ with مَثَل means to present or set forth an example, but the root meaning of ضَرَبَ is to strike or hit. This suggests an example that makes an impact, that strikes the consciousness and gets their attention.

أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ—"the people of the town." Allah ﷻ deliberately doesn't specify which town. There has been much speculation among scholars: Which town was this? Who were these messengers? What time period? Were these prophets sent directly by Allah ﷻ, or were they messengers sent by a prophet, perhaps disciples from the time of 'Isa (AS)?

The fact that Allah ﷻ doesn't name the town or the messengers is itself significant. He wants us to focus on the event itself, on the pattern of behavior, because this pattern duplicates itself throughout history. The specifics matter less than the universal lesson.

إِذْ جَآءَهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ—"when the messengers came to it." The word مُرْسَلُون is from the root ر-س-ل, meaning "to send." It's plural, indicating more than two. The dominant opinion among classical scholars is that these were actual messengers of Allah ﷻ, prophets sent to guide this community. The use of the plural is significant—this wasn't a single messenger, but multiple messengers sent to the same town, emphasizing the gravity of the message and the extent of Allah's mercy in sending guidance.

translation: When We sent to them two but they denied them, so We strengthened them with a third, and they said, "Indeed, we are messengers to you
commentary:
إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱثْنَيْنِ فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا فَعَزَّزْنَا بِثَالِثٍ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُم مُّرْسَلُونَ

*When We sent to them two, but they denied them, so We strengthened them with a third. And they said, "Indeed, we are messengers to you."*

إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱثْنَيْنِ—"When We sent to them two." Allah ﷻ initially sent two messengers, establishing the message through multiple witnesses.

فَكَذَّبُوهُمَا—"But they called them both liars." The form كَذَّبُوا is intensive, meaning they didn't just disbelieve—they actively and emphatically rejected them as liars.

فَعَزَّزْنَا بِثَالِثٍ—"So We strengthened them with a third." The verb عَزَّزَ means to strengthen, honor, dignify, and fortify. By sending a third messenger, Allah ﷻ reinforced the message, gave additional strength to the mission, and left the people with even less excuse for rejection.

فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُم مُّرْسَلُونَ—"And they said, 'Indeed, we are messengers to you.'" United, the three messengers delivered their message together with one voice. The particle إِنَّ emphasizes the certainty of their claim.

translation: They said, "You are not but human beings like us, and the Most Merciful has not revealed a thing. You are only telling lies
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مِن شَىْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ

*They said, "You are not but human beings like us, and the Most Merciful has not revealed anything. You are only telling lies."*

قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا—"They said, 'You are nothing but human beings like us.'" This is one of the most common objections raised against messengers throughout history. "You're just flesh and skin like us, mortal beings no different from ourselves. How can you claim to bring a message from God?"

The word بَشَر emphasizes the physical, human aspect—skin, flesh, mortality. The objection reveals a fundamental misunderstanding: they couldn't conceive that Allah ﷻ would choose someone from among themselves to deliver His message.

وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مِن شَىْءٍ—"And the Most Merciful has not sent down anything." They denied the very possibility of revelation. Notice that even while denying the message, they use the name الرَّحْمَـٰن, suggesting they acknowledged Allah ﷻ but rejected that He would communicate with humans through revelation.

إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا تَكْذِبُونَ—"You are nothing but liars." They dismissed the messengers completely, accusing them of fabricating the entire message.

translation: They said, "Our Lord knows that we are messengers to you
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا يَعْلَمُ إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمُرْسَلُونَ

*They said, "Our Lord knows that we are messengers to you."*

قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا يَعْلَمُ—"They said, 'Our Lord knows.'" The messengers' response is profound in its simplicity. They don't argue, don't get defensive, don't try to prove themselves to the people. They simply state: Our Master knows.

إِنَّآ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمُرْسَلُونَ—"Indeed, we are certainly messengers to you." The particle لَ adds emphasis. In other words: We don't need validation from you. We already have validation from the One who sent us. Allah ﷻ has given us our credentials, and that's sufficient.

This parallels the situation of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Allah ﷻ validated him repeatedly in the Quran: you are indeed from among the messengers, you are upon guidance. The messenger doesn't require human approval when he has divine appointment. This is a powerful lesson for anyone carrying the message of truth—your validation comes from Allah ﷻ, not from whether people accept you.

translation: And we are not responsible except for clear notification
commentary:
وَمَا عَلَيْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

*And there is not upon us except the clear notification.*

وَمَا عَلَيْنَآ إِلَّا—"And nothing is upon us except..." This restricts their responsibility to one thing only.

ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ—"the clear delivery." The word بَلَاغ means to reach, to arrive, to deliver. From it comes the concept of communication—making words reach the intended recipient. But بَلَاغ takes on a deeper meaning in Arabic. It refers to words that are so powerful they penetrate the fortress of the chest and reach deep inside to touch the heart.

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ: قُل لَّهُمْ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ قَوْلًۢا بَلِيغًا (Say to them a word that reaches them deep in their souls). This is بَلِيغ—eloquent, penetrating speech. It's the image of words breaking through the chest cavity that protects the heart. Usually when someone speaks, you can choose not to let those words affect you, to keep them at surface level. But when words are بَلِيغ, they pierce through and enter the heart.

So the messengers' responsibility is to deliver words that are not just clear (مُبِين), but powerful enough to reach the depths of the soul. The responsibility for how people respond to those words rests with the listeners, not the messengers. The messengers' obligation is only الْبَلَاغُ الْمُبِينُ—clear, powerful delivery of the message.

translation: They said, "Indeed, we consider you a bad omen. If you do not desist, we will surely stone you, and there will surely touch you, from us, a painful punishment
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنَا بِكُمْ ۖ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهُوا۟ لَنَرْجُمَنَّكُمْ وَلَيَمَسَّنَّكُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

*They said, "Indeed, we consider you a bad omen. If you do not desist, we will surely stone you, and there will surely touch you from us a painful punishment."*

قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنَا بِكُمْ—"They said, 'Indeed, we consider you a bad omen.'" The root ط-ي-ر relates to birds (طَيْر). The verb تَطَيَّرَ means to see something as a bad omen based on birds—similar to how various cultures, both ancient and modern, consider certain birds like crows as signs of bad luck or death.

The people attributed their problems to the presence of the messengers. "The economic difficulties we're facing, the troubles in our community—it's all because you people are here. You're cursed, and you've brought this curse upon us."

This is a pattern we see repeatedly when messengers come to a town. Allah ﷻ often brings some difficulty to that community—economic hardship, drought, reduced luxuries—so that they might soften, reflect, and turn back to Him. But rather than see these challenges as a wake-up call, the people blame the messengers. "Our gods are telling us you're the problem."

لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهُوا۟ لَنَرْجُمَنَّكُمْ—"If you don't stop, we will stone you to death." The threat escalates to violence. They're not interested in dialogue or evidence—they want the message silenced, permanently.

وَلَيَمَسَّنَّكُم مِّنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—"And a painful punishment will certainly touch you from us." The alternative to stoning is torture. Both threats are designed to intimidate the messengers into silence.

translation: They said, "Your omen is with yourselves. Is it because you were reminded? Rather, you are a transgressing people
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ طَـٰٓئِرُكُم مَّعَكُمْ ۚ أَئِن ذُكِّرْتُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ

*They said, "Your omen is with yourselves. Is it because you were reminded? Rather, you are a transgressing people."*

قَالُوا۟ طَـٰٓئِرُكُم مَّعَكُمْ—"They said, 'Your bad omen is with you.'" The messengers turn the accusation back. "This curse you talk about, this bad luck—it's something you've made up. It stays with you. You can keep that nonsense to yourselves."

أَئِن ذُكِّرْتُم—"Is it because you were reminded?" Even when you're being given a powerful reminder, when you're being shown clear guidance, all you can think about is some baseless curse? Your response to clear truth is to invent superstitions?

بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ—"Rather, you are a people who go overboard." The word مُسْرِف means one who exceeds limits, who goes to excess. The messengers identify the root problem: excess. Your lifestyles are excessive, your reactions are excessive and poorly thought out, your materialism is excessive, and even your response to us is excessive. You jump immediately to violence and threats. Everything you do lacks balance and moderation.

This diagnosis is profound. A people trapped in excess cannot hear the message of balance and divine guidance. Their entire framework is distorted by extremes.

translation: And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running. He said, "O my people, follow the messengers
commentary:
وَجَآءَ مِنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعَىٰ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

*And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running. He said, "O my people, follow the messengers."*

وَجَآءَ مِنْ أَقْصَا ٱلْمَدِينَةِ رَجُلٌ يَسْعَىٰ—"And from the farthest end of the city came a man, running." The word أَقْصَى means the farthest point, the extreme end. This could indicate geographical distance—he came from the outskirts of the city—but it likely also suggests socioeconomic distance. He came from the poor side of town, from the ghettos, from the margins of society.

The word يَسْعَىٰ means running, hurrying with purpose. He didn't stroll casually—he rushed because the situation was urgent.

This man came specifically to stand with the messengers. He heard what was happening and ran to support them. But he's coming from the wrong side of town to speak to the elite, to the powerful. This means they have little respect for what he has to say. In fact, they're likely offended that someone of his social standing would dare address them.

قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ—"He said, 'O my people, follow the messengers.'" Despite his low social status, despite the danger, despite knowing they look down on him, he speaks up. يَـٰقَوْمِ—"O my people"—he claims them as his people, expressing his genuine concern for their welfare. He's not condemning them from a distance; he's appealing to them as one of their own.

translation: Follow those who do not ask of you [any] payment, and they are [rightly] guided
commentary:
ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَن لَّا يَسْـَٔلُكُمْ أَجْرًا وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ

*Follow those who do not ask you for payment, and they are rightly guided.*

ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَن لَّا يَسْـَٔلُكُمْ أَجْرًا—"Follow those who don't ask you for any compensation." This reveals something about the audience. There wouldn't be any assumption that the messengers want money if they were speaking to people who had no money. So this confirms the messengers were addressing people of wealth, and those people assumed—as wealthy people often do—"These religious figures just want some of our money. That's why they're giving us these lectures."

The man from the poor end of town reminds them: these messengers aren't asking you for anything material. They have no financial motive, no hidden agenda to enrich themselves at your expense.

وَهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ—"And they are guided ones." Not only are they sincere, but they're upon true guidance. Their character speaks for itself. You know these people already. You've observed them. You've seen their integrity. Their lives testify to their truthfulness.

translation: And why should I not worship He who created me and to whom you will be returned
commentary:
وَمَا لِىَ لَآ أَعْبُدُ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

*And why should I not worship He who created me, and to Him you will be returned?*

وَمَا لِىَ لَآ أَعْبُدُ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى—"And what would be wrong with me that I wouldn't worship the One who originated me?" The verb فَطَرَ means to originate, to create from nothing, to mold and fashion. It emphasizes the originality and perfection of creation.

This man shifts the argument to the most fundamental truth: his own existence. He's saying, "Even if you reject the messengers, even if you doubt their message, how can I deny my own creation? When I reflect on the fact that I've been fashioned, molded, and created by Allah ﷻ—isn't that alone sufficient reason to worship Him?"

This is powerful reasoning. He's not relying on the messengers' authority or on complex theological arguments. He's pointing to something undeniable: our own existence and the question of who created us.

وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ—"And to Him you will all be returned." He doesn't say "I will be returned"—he says تُرْجَعُونَ, "you (plural) will be returned." He's including them in this reality. Whether you accept it or not, whether you believe it or not, you're going back to Him. This is your destiny as much as it is mine.

translation: Should I take other than Him [false] deities [while], if the Most Merciful intends for me some adversity, their intercession will not avail me at all, nor can they save me
commentary:
**ءَأَتَّخِذُ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً إِن يُرِدْنِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ بِضُرٍّ لَّا تُغْنِ عَنِّى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يُنقِذُونِ**

The believer's argument reaches its climax with a profound rhetorical question: Should I take gods besides Him? He forces his people to confront the reality of their false worship through practical reasoning. If الرحمن, the Most Merciful, wanted any harm to come to me, would the intercession of these supposed gods benefit me at all? 

The answer is devastatingly clear: لَّا تُغْنِ عَنِّى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا (their intercession would not benefit me in anything). These gods they rely upon couldn't come to his aid at all. The phrase وَلَا يُنقِذُونِ (nor can they rescue me) emphasizes their complete powerlessness. They cannot ward off punishment, they cannot save him from harm, they cannot protect him in any meaningful way.

Notice the deliberate choice of the name Rahman in this argument. Even the Most Merciful, if He willed harm, cannot be opposed by these false deities. This highlights both Allah's ﷻ absolute power and His mercy, since He rarely exercises His power to harm, being Rahman. Yet even in His mercy, He remains supreme over all creation.

translation: Indeed, I would then be in manifest error
commentary:
**إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ**

The believer completes his logical argument: إِنِّىٓ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (I would then be in clear misguidance). If he were to worship these powerless gods, if he were to place his trust in beings that cannot protect or benefit, he would be lost in obvious error. The word ضَلَـٰلٍ means to be lost, to wander without direction, to be completely misguided.

This statement demonstrates intellectual honesty and clarity of thought. He's not simply making an emotional appeal; he's walking through the logic step by step, showing how worship of anything besides Allah ﷻ leads inevitably to clear misguidance.

translation: Indeed, I have believed in your Lord, so listen to me
commentary:
**إِنِّىٓ ءَامَنتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ فَٱسْمَعُونِ**

After dismantling the false logic of shirk, he makes his declaration: إِنِّىٓ ءَامَنتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ (I have believed in your Master). Then he adds فَٱسْمَعُونِ (so listen to me).

Notice the remarkable wisdom in his approach throughout this entire speech. He speaks constantly about himself. Every statement centers on what he believes, what he would do, what would happen to him. He never once says, "You people commit shirk. You people are misguided. You people do this or that." 

This teaches us a profound lesson about calling others to truth. When trying to reach people's hearts, talk about yourself, not about them. The messengers can address people directly because they carry divine authority, but this believer was not a messenger. He came to support the messengers. He couldn't talk down to people; he had to speak on his own behalf. "This is what's going on with me. I want to share with you what's happening with me, my own struggle."

This approach is brilliant because it takes the guard off the listener. Even in lectures, classes or conversations about improving ourselves, we should make it personal: "I should pray better. I should get up early. I should stop backbiting. I should control my temper." Not "you" or "the youth" or "some Muslims," but "I should do it." When the discourse is dominated by "I," people's defenses come down.

But these people were at a different level. When they heard his plea to listen, their response was violent. They murdered him. They killed him for speaking the truth.

translation: It was said, "Enter Paradise." He said, "I wish my people could know
commentary:
**قِيلَ ٱدْخُلِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ ۖ قَالَ يَـٰلَيْتَ قَوْمِى يَعْلَمُونَ**

Immediately after his declaration of faith, we read قِيلَ ٱدْخُلِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ (it was said: "Enter Jannah"). How does he enter Jannah from right there? What happened in between? He was killed. The Quran doesn't even mention his murder because it's insignificant compared to what follows.

This teaches us something profound about what's being conveyed in this surah: be ready to be sacrificed for truth. And if you are killed in this path, Allah ﷻ won't even mention the killing because you won't even remember it. All you'll remember are the words ٱدْخُلِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ (Enter Jannah).

The shaheed, the martyr, doesn't have to wait until Judgment Day. He was given direct access. He goes straight to Jannah. He called to Allah ﷻ and that was his crime, and he was killed, and immediately he's told to enter Paradise.

You can imagine his family crying, loved ones weeping, everyone around him in shock and grief. But his response? يَـٰلَيْتَ قَوْمِى يَعْلَمُونَ (Oh, if only my people knew). Even after being murdered, even after being granted entry to Jannah, his first thought is for his people. He wishes they could understand what he now understands.

translation: Of how my Lord has forgiven me and placed me among the honored
commentary:
**بِمَا غَفَرَ لِى رَبِّى وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ**

What does he wish they knew? بِمَا غَفَرَ لِى رَبِّى (of what my Master has forgiven me) وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ (and made me from those who have been honored). He wishes they could see the forgiveness Allah ﷻ granted him, the honor bestowed upon him. His concern remains for those he left behind, hoping they would recognize the reality of what he discovered.

translation: And We did not send down upon his people after him any soldiers from the heaven, nor would We have done so
commentary:
**۞ وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِن جُندٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا كُنَّا مُنزِلِينَ**

His struggle is now over. He came running, he spoke the truth, he paid the price. As the Quran says elsewhere, "Among the believers are men who have been true to their covenant with Allah ﷻ. Among them is he who has fulfilled his vow, and among them is he who awaits." This believer has already fulfilled his vow and departed. But what happened to those left behind?

وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِن جُندٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (We didn't send upon his people after him any army from the sky) وَمَا كُنَّا مُنزِلِينَ (and We were not going to send one). They didn't deserve a grand punishment. No army was necessary to deal with them. They weren't significant enough to warrant such a response.

translation: It was not but one shout, and immediately they were extinguished
commentary:
**إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ خَـٰمِدُونَ**

إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً (It was nothing but a single cry) فَإِذَا هُمْ خَـٰمِدُونَ (and immediately they were extinguished). One loud scream, one blast of sound, and they were lying there lifeless.

The word خَـٰمِدُونَ describes a fire becoming weak and dying down. It refers to the fire of life inside a person fading away. They died slowly. They were paralyzed and couldn't help themselves. Slowly but surely, the fire of life was burning out of them. Bodies lying around, no help coming, no rescue arriving. They're just watching as they fall to the ground, watching their lives leave their bodies.

The word خمود (khumud) also refers to when fever goes down, when heat dissipates. Bodies get cold when they die, and slowly but surely their bodies grew cold. This is the scene being described: a slow and painful death, a horrible end for this nation that rejected the truth and murdered the one who spoke it.

translation: How regretful for the servants. There did not come to them any messenger except that they used to ridicule him
commentary:
**يَـٰحَسْرَةً عَلَى ٱلْعِبَادِ ۚ مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ**

يَـٰحَسْرَةً عَلَى ٱلْعِبَادِ (Oh, what regret upon the slaves). Allah ﷻ expresses what terrible regret, what profound sorrow for these servants. مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (There has not come to them any messenger except that they used to ridicule him).

This is the tragic pattern of human history. Messenger after messenger came with clear guidance, and the response was mockery, ridicule, rejection. This pattern repeats across nations and eras, bringing regret upon those who refuse to listen.

translation: Have they not considered how many generations We destroyed before them - that they to them will not return
commentary:
**أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ**

أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ (Have they not seen how many We destroyed before them of the generations?) أَنَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ (that they will not return to them).

Haven't they observed how many civilizations, how many different kinds of towns and societies We've already annihilated? Once they're gone, they're gone. They become ancient history, stories people visit as tourists. People take pictures at pyramids and ruins, but they weren't there when destruction came. These mighty civilizations are now just remnants, unable to return to warn those who come after.

translation: And indeed, all of them will yet be brought present before Us
commentary:
**وَإِن كُلٌّ لَّمَّا جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ**

وَإِن كُلٌّ لَّمَّا جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ (And indeed, every single one of them, all together, will be brought before Us). Despite being scattered across history, despite becoming dust and memory, absolutely every single one of them will be gathered on the Day when all of humanity stands before Allah ﷻ. They will be presented, brought forward to give account.

translation: And a sign for them is the dead earth. We have brought it to life and brought forth from it grain, and from it they eat
commentary:
**وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ أَحْيَيْنَـٰهَا وَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهَا حَبًّا فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ**

The discourse now shifts dramatically. There are two kinds of evidence: what is behind us and what is in front of us. What is behind us is history, and we've just been given evidence from history through this powerful story. Now Allah ﷻ directs our attention to what is in front of us: nature, the earth, the observable world around us.

وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ (And a sign for them is the dead earth). Another miraculous sign for them exists in the lifeless earth. أَحْيَيْنَـٰهَا (We gave it life) وَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهَا حَبًّا (and We brought forth from it grain) فَمِنْهُ يَأْكُلُونَ (from which they eat).

The dead earth receives rain and springs to life, producing grain and seed that becomes farmland, providing sustenance for humanity. This simple, observable miracle happens before our eyes constantly. We give life to dead earth, draw out grain from it, and from it people consume and survive.

Allah ﷻ has turned our attention first to a story that represents the struggle of all prophets throughout history—that's what's behind us. Now He calls us to reflect on what's in front of us: the signs in nature that we should observe and contemplate. These lessons continue to unfold in the ayahs that follow.

translation: And We placed therein gardens of palm trees and grapevines and caused to burst forth therefrom some springs
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا جَنَّـٰتٍ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَأَعْنَـٰبٍ وَفَجَّرْنَا فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْعُيُونِ

"And We placed therein gardens of date palms and grapevines, and We caused springs to gush forth within them."

Allah ﷻ continues describing His signs in the earth, specifying how He has made gardens flourish with date palms and grapevines. The word فَجَّرْنَا carries the imagery of springs exploding forth, gushing out from the land. These springs provide fresh water that allows vegetation to grow even on mountainsides, producing all kinds of magnificent fruit. The water cascades down, creating waterfalls and springs that sustain life and beauty throughout the landscape.

translation: That they may eat of His fruit. And their hands have not produced it, so will they not be grateful
commentary:
لِيَأْكُلُوا۟ مِن ثَمَرِهِۦ وَمَا عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ

"So they may eat of its fruit, and their hands have not produced it. So will they not be grateful?"

Allah ﷻ reveals the purpose behind these gardens and springs: so that human beings can eat from the fruit they produce. Then comes a remarkable observation: وَمَا عَمِلَتْهُ أَيْدِيهِمْ - "and their hands have not produced it." They didn't make the oranges round. They didn't color them. They didn't create their taste or texture. All of this intricate design and delicious result comes without their hands crafting it. 

After presenting this clear evidence of divine provision, Allah ﷻ asks: أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُون - "So will they not be grateful?" The question hangs in the air, inviting reflection on how much we take for granted the elaborate systems Allah ﷻ has put in place for our nourishment and pleasure.

translation: Exalted is He who created all pairs - from what the earth grows and from themselves and from that which they do not know
commentary:
سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمِمَّا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

"Glory be to Him who created all pairs - from what the earth grows and from themselves and from that which they do not know."

سُبْحَانَ declares the absolute perfection of Allah ﷻ, the One who created all pairs, all spouses, every single one of them. This pairing exists in three realms that Allah ﷻ mentions: first, مِمَّا تُنۢبِتُ ٱلْأَرْضُ - from what sprouts from the earth, the male and female in plants. Second, وَمِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ - from their own selves, meaning human beings created in pairs. And third, وَمِمَّا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - from species and creatures they don't even know about.

This last category is particularly profound. There are forms of life that exist in pairs that we haven't even discovered yet. Allah ﷻ is telling us that His creative pattern of pairing extends far beyond what we can observe or imagine. The perfection of this design deserves our declaration of His glory.

translation: And a sign for them is the night. We remove from it [the light of] day, so they are [left] in darkness
commentary:
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمُ ٱلَّيْلُ نَسْلَخُ مِنْهُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فَإِذَا هُم مُّظْلِمُونَ

"And a sign for them is the night. We remove from it [the light of] day, so they are [left] in darkness."

The ayah shifts to another miracle: the night itself. The verb نَسْلَخُ means to peel, to shed skin like a snake, or to remove the hide from an animal. It's also used when a deadline expires: سَلَخَ الشَّهْرُ - the month ran out. 

Allah ﷻ describes removing the day from the night as though He is yanking a cover off, pulling a sheet from a bed. The imagery is vivid and powerful. The day acts as a covering, and when Allah ﷻ removes it, فَإِذَا هُم مُّظْلِمُونَ - then suddenly they are left in darkness. Night isn't simply the absence of day; it's what remains when the day's sheet is pulled away.

translation: And the sun runs [on course] toward its stopping point. That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing
commentary:
وَٱلشَّمْسُ تَجْرِى لِمُسْتَقَرٍّ لَّهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ

"And the sun runs [on course] toward its stopping point. That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing."

The sun تَجْرِى - it continues to swim, to float in a position that is appointed for it. The word مُسْتَقَرّ indicates a time, a situation, a position that has been precisely designated. This is not random motion but calculated, ordained movement.

Allah ﷻ concludes by attributing this to His names: ذَٰلِكَ تَقْدِيرُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْعَلِيمِ - "That is the calculation and decree of the Exalted in Might, the All-Knowing." Al-'Azeez speaks to His ultimate authority and power, while Al-'Aleem affirms His perfect knowledge. The sun's course is both a demonstration of divine power and divine knowledge working in perfect harmony.

translation: And the moon - We have determined for it phases, until it returns [appearing] like the old date stalk
commentary:
وَٱلْقَمَرَ قَدَّرْنَـٰهُ مَنَازِلَ حَتَّىٰ عَادَ كَٱلْعُرْجُونِ ٱلْقَدِيمِ

"And the moon - We have determined for it phases, until it returns [appearing] like the old, curved date palm stalk."

Allah ﷻ turns our attention to the moon. قَدَّرْنَـٰهُ مَنَازِل - We calculated, decreed, and dedicated its stages, its phases. The moon doesn't just wander through the sky randomly; it follows precisely ordained phases.

حَتَّىٰ عَادَ كَٱلْعُرْجُونِ ٱلْقَدِيمِ - "until it returns like the old, curved date palm stalk." The عُرْجُون refers to the bent pieces of bark that stick out from date palms. When you take one of these pieces out, it has a distinctive arch that closely resembles the crescent moon. Allah ﷻ uses an image from the Arabian landscape that would have been immediately familiar to His first audience, yet the comparison remains striking and accurate for anyone who observes it.

translation: It is not allowable for the sun to reach the moon, nor does the night overtake the day, but each, in an orbit, is swimming
commentary:
لَا ٱلشَّمْسُ يَنۢبَغِى لَهَآ أَن تُدْرِكَ ٱلْقَمَرَ وَلَا ٱلَّيْلُ سَابِقُ ٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ وَكُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ

"It is not allowable for the sun to reach the moon, nor does the night overtake the day, but each, in an orbit, is swimming."

لَا يَنۢبَغِى - it is not becoming, not appropriate, not allowable for the sun to catch up to or overtake the moon. Each celestial body has its designated path and time. Similarly, the night cannot get ahead of the day. There is perfect order and timing in Allah's creation.

وَكُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ - "and each, in an orbit, is swimming." The phrase كُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ is actually a palindrome in Arabic: kaf-lam-fa, fa-lam-kaf, with the ya in the middle. The letters read the same forwards and backwards, just as the orbits themselves operate in perfect symmetry. The word choice itself mirrors the cosmic reality it describes. They swim through space in their designated paths, never colliding, never interfering with each other's course.

translation: And a sign for them is that We carried their forefathers in a laden ship
commentary:
وَءَايَةٌ لَّهُمْ أَنَّا حَمَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ

"And a sign for them is that We carried their forefathers in the laden ship."

Allah ﷻ presents another sign: أَنَّا حَمَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ - that We boarded them, even their children and forefathers, onto ships that are fully loaded. This references the ship of Nuh (AS), but it also speaks to the general miracle of sea travel. The ability to board massive vessels with entire families and cargo, to traverse dangerous waters safely, is itself a sign of Allah's mercy and design.

translation: And We created for them from the likes of it that which they ride
commentary:
وَخَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ مَا يَرْكَبُونَ

"And We created for them from the likes of it that which they ride."

وَخَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّن مِّثْلِهِ - "And We created for them from the likes of it." Allah ﷻ has already designed, in His architectural wisdom, things that resemble ships that human beings ride upon: planes, helicopters, submarines. They board these vessels with their children, traveling through air and under water just as the ship travels on the surface. The principle of transportation, of being carried safely through dangerous elements, extends across multiple forms of travel.

translation: And if We should will, We could drown them; then no one responding to a cry would there be for them, nor would they be saved
commentary:
وَإِن نَّشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ فَلَا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنقَذُونَ

"And if We should will, We could drown them; then no one responding to a cry would there be for them, nor would they be saved."

وَإِن نَّشَأْ نُغْرِقْهُمْ - if Allah ﷻ wanted, He could sink them all. فَلَا صَرِيخَ لَهُمْ - then there would be no one they could cry out to in distress. The word صَرِيخ refers to crying out for help in desperation. This echoes the scene from Surah Al-Qasas when the man who had asked Musa (AS) for help the previous day was calling out again: يَسْتَصْرِخُهُ.

وَلَا هُمْ يُنقَذُونَ - and they would not be rescued at all. When you're in the middle of the ocean and something goes wrong, there's no lifeguard station nearby. Complete helplessness would overtake them if Allah ﷻ willed it.

translation: Except as a mercy from Us and provision for a time
commentary:
إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

"Except as a mercy from Us and provision for a time."

إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا - except this safety, this successful journey, is purely a mercy from Allah. وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ - and as an enjoyment, a provision until an appointed time.

This ayah transforms how a believer should view travel. When you board a plane to Chicago, to New York, to anywhere, and you land safely, you recognize this is only through Allah's mercy. Even though you may have traveled for vacation, to meet family, or to conduct business, these enjoyments are only for a limited time - إِلَىٰ حِينٍ.

Just as this particular journey came to an end and you reached safe shore, the journey of life itself will also come to an end. Allah ﷻ uses the completion of a physical journey to remind us of the temporary nature of our entire existence in this world.

translation: But when it is said to them, "Beware of what is before you and what is behind you; perhaps you will receive mercy
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَمَا خَلْفَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

"But when it is said to them, 'Beware of what is before you and what is behind you that you may receive mercy...'"

When these disbelievers are told: ٱتَّقُوا۟ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيكُمْ - take precaution, be cautious of what is in front of you. This has multiple meanings. It refers to the revelation that's in front of them, the message that has been presented to them. It also means: be careful about what lies ahead in your future, the consequences that await.

وَمَا خَلْفَكُمْ - and what is behind you. This refers to the messages that came before them, the history of previous nations who rejected their messengers, and the signs that have already passed. Take heed from what came before and what lies ahead, لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ - so that you may receive mercy.

translation: And no sign comes to them from the signs of their Lord except that they are from it turning away
commentary:
وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ

"And there comes not to them a sign from among the signs of their Lord except that they are, from it, turning away."

Despite all the signs Allah ﷻ has presented - the gardens, the springs, the night and day, the sun and moon, the ships - وَمَا تَأْتِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ - not a single miracle from any of the miracles of their Master comes to them, إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا مُعْرِضِينَ - except their attitude remains the same. They deliberately continue to ignore them, turning away with willful disregard.

The word مُعْرِضِينَ implies active avoidance, not passive ignorance. They see the signs and consciously choose to turn their faces away from them.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Spend from that which Allah has provided for you," those who disbelieve say to those who believe, "Should we feed one whom, if Allah had willed, He would have fed? You are not but in clear error
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنُطْعِمُ مَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ أَطْعَمَهُۥٓ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

"And when it is said to them, 'Spend from that which Allah has provided for you,' those who disbelieve say to those who believe, 'Should we feed one whom, if Allah had willed, He would have fed? You are not but in clear error.'"

When told: أَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ - spend from what Allah ﷻ has provided you, the disbelievers respond with twisted logic. They say to the believers with dripping sarcasm: أَنُطْعِمُ مَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ أَطْعَمَهُۥٓ - "Should we feed someone who, if your God - this God you claim is so great, who made all these fruits and trees and magnificent things - if He wanted to feed them, He would have fed them Himself. Why should we feed them?"

They use sarcasm as a clever response to avoid giving charity. إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ - "You people are in such obvious confusion. First you talk about this great God who provides, and then there are all these poor people in the world. What kind of God is that?"

This is the same tired argument that continues today. Those who think themselves intellectually superior show up with philosophical objections: "Why isn't your God feeding the starving children? What's going on?" They hear the message but respond with mockery rather than reflection.

translation: And they say, "When is this promise, if you should be truthful
commentary:
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

"And they say, 'When is this promise, if you should be truthful?'"

After mocking the call to charity, they shift to mocking the promise of accountability: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ - "When is this promise coming? إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ - if in fact you're telling the truth, when is Judgment Day?"

The year 2000 came and went. Various apocalyptic predictions have come and passed. They point to these failed predictions as if they invalidate the divine promise, demonstrating their fundamental misunderstanding of how Allah's timing works.

translation: They do not await except one blast which will seize them while they are disputing
commentary:
مَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ

"They do not await except one blast which will seize them while they are disputing."

مَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً - they're not going to be staring at anything, waiting for anything, except one loud scream, one blast. تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ - that will seize them while they are in the midst of their obnoxious debating, their pointless arguing back and forth.

In the middle of their disputes and mockery, in the middle of their daily arguments and business dealings, the blast will catch them completely off guard.

translation: And they will not be able [to give] any instruction, nor to their people can they return
commentary:
فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً وَلَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ

"And they will not be able [to give] any instruction, nor to their people can they return."

فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ تَوْصِيَةً - they won't even be able to leave a will. The word تَوْصِيَةً also carries the meaning of those emergency moments when you call someone during a crisis: "There's an earthquake. In case I don't make it, take care of the kids. If anything happens, just know I love you."

There will be no time for goodbyes. وَلَآ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمْ يَرْجِعُونَ - and they won't be able to return to their families for one last embrace, one final moment together. Everything will happen so suddenly that there's no opportunity for any of these final human gestures.

translation: And the Horn will be blown; and at once from the graves to their Lord they will hasten
commentary:
وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَإِذَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَنسِلُونَ

"And the Horn will be blown, and at once from the graves to their Lord they will hasten."

وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ - and the trumpet will be blown into. This is the second trumpet. Remember, the first trumpet causes everything to die and collapse. Between the first and second trumpet, everything is in a state of complete unconsciousness - a true sleep. But before the first trumpet, from the time of death until that first blast, people are awake in their graves, being rewarded or punished, the believers enjoying breezes from Jannah and visiting each other.

This ayah describes the second trumpet, the trumpet of resurrection. فَإِذَا هُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ - then suddenly they emerge from their graves. The word أَجْدَاث specifically refers to graves that have no markings left, graves so old you wouldn't even know they were there. Suddenly, in the middle of an empty field, people start emerging from graves that were buried thousands of years ago.

إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَنسِلُونَ - toward their Lord they descend down, heading downward. The verb يَنسِلُونَ suggests flowing downward like water trickling down a slope, all ending up in the same place. Allah ﷻ will stretch the entire earth into a slope, and everybody pours down toward the gathering place, moving inexorably toward their Lord.

translation: They will say, "O woe to us! Who has raised us up from our sleeping place?" [The reply will be], "This is what the Most Merciful had promised, and the messengers told the truth
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا مَنۢ بَعَثَنَا مِن مَّرْقَدِنَا ۜ ۗ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَصَدَقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ

"They will say, 'O woe to us! Who has raised us from our resting place?' [The reply will be], 'This is what the Most Merciful had promised, and the messengers told the truth.'"

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا - they will cry out, "O, what horrible thing has happened to us!" The word وَيْل expresses that nothing worse could possibly happen. Things couldn't possibly be worse than this moment.

مَنۢ بَعَثَنَا مِن مَّرْقَدِنَا - "Who woke us up from our sleep?" The word مَرْقَد means a light sleep. This is because between the first and second trumpet, there was indeed a state of sleep - complete unconsciousness. They're waking up from that period between the two trumpet blasts.

Then reality hits them: هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ - "This is what the Most Merciful promised!" It's all coming back to them now. Everything they were warned about, everything they mocked. وَصَدَقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ - "And those messengers we ridiculed and dismissed were telling the truth all along."

translation: It will not be but one blast, and at once they are all brought present before Us
commentary:
إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ

"It will not be but one blast, and at once they are all brought present before Us."

إِن كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً - it's going to be nothing but one loud scream, one blast. فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَّدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ - then all of them together, in unison, are going to be standing before Us, presented before Allah ﷻ.

The speed and totality of the gathering is emphasized. One blast, and immediately the entire creation from all of human history stands assembled.

translation: So today no soul will be wronged at all, and you will not be recompensed except for what you used to do
commentary:
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

"So Today no soul will be wronged at all, and you will not be recompensed except for what you used to do."

فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا - on that Day, not a single soul, no individual, is going to be wronged in any way. Not even the smallest injustice will occur.

وَلَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - and none of you are going to be given anything except exactly what you worked for. You will receive precisely what you earned, nothing more and nothing less. Perfect justice will be established.

translation: Indeed the companions of Paradise, that Day, will be amused in [joyful] occupation
commentary:
إِنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى شُغُلٍ فَـٰكِهُونَ

"Indeed the companions of Paradise, that Day, will be amused in [joyful] occupation."

After describing the terror of the disbelievers, Allah ﷻ shifts dramatically to the believers: إِنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ - indeed, the companions of Jannah. The word أَصْحَـٰب doesn't just mean "people of" - it means people who want to be in its company, who never want to leave. When you've been somewhere too long, you want to leave. But not the people of Jannah. They never want to be anywhere else.

ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى شُغُلٍ - on that Day they will be in activities, busy doing things. شُغُل means you're occupied, immersed in something. They're engaged in all sorts of enjoyable pursuits.

فَـٰكِهُونَ - they're so happy doing it. The word فَاكِه comes from the same root as فَاكِهَة, fruit - something that puts a smile on your face. They're engaged in activities that bring them pure joy, one after another, without exhaustion, without disappointment, without waiting in line. Every activity is amazing, and there's infinite time to experience it all.

translation: They and their spouses - in shade, reclining on adorned couches
commentary:
هُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُمْ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ مُتَّكِـُٔونَ

"They and their spouses - in shade, reclining on adorned couches."

هُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُمْ - they and their spouses together. فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ - in beautiful shades. عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ  - upon couches and comfortable furnishings. مُتَّكِـُٔونَ - reclining back in complete relaxation.

Even when they want to take a break from all those amazing activities, they sit with their beloved spouse in perfect shade, on the most comfortable furniture imaginable. "Isn't this amazing?" "Yes, this is incredible." The scenery is breathtaking, the comfort is absolute, and they're sharing it with the one they love most.

translation: For them therein is fruit, and for them is whatever they request [or wish]
commentary:
لَهُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ وَلَهُم مَّا يَدَّعُونَ

"For them therein is fruit, and for them is whatever they request."

لَهُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ - they will have fruits presented to them in that shade. Fruits that make them smile. Something that looks like strawberries, but when you bite it, it's something completely different. "You have to try this!" And the next one is entirely unique as well. The Quran mentions elsewhere that the people of Jannah will say, "We were given something like this before," but it's only made to look similar - each taste is brand new and extraordinary.

وَلَهُم مَّا يَدَّعُونَ - and for them is whatever they call for, whatever they request. After enjoying the fruit, maybe they think, "I wonder if they have that incredible dish I loved in this world?" And instantly, it appears - but the Jannah version, which is beyond comparison. Whatever your memory recalls from this world, whatever you desire, whatever you call for appears immediately. And these requests aren't limited to food - they extend to anything you ever wanted.

translation: [And] "Peace," a word from a Merciful Lord
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْلًا مِّن رَّبٍّ رَّحِيمٍ

"[And] 'Peace,' a word from a Merciful Lord."

سَلَـٰمٌ - Peace. Complete peace, perfect peace. قَوْلًا مِّن رَّبٍّ رَّحِيمٍ - a word, a greeting, a declaration from a Lord who is Merciful.

This is the ultimate gift. Beyond all the activities, the fruit, the companionship, the comfort - there is the word of peace directly from Allah ﷻ, from the Lord who is Rahman and Raheem. This divine greeting surpasses every other blessing of Jannah. It's not just safety from fear or anxiety - it's the profound peace that comes from being in the presence of your Lord, hearing His words of peace addressed to you directly.

translation: [Then He will say], "But stand apart today, you criminals
commentary:
وَٱمْتَـٰزُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ

After addressing the believers with peace, Allah ﷻ now commands: "Distinguish yourselves. Separate yourselves today, O you criminals." The verb امْتَازُوا means to be set apart, to be distinguished from others. On this Day, a clear line will be drawn between the believers and the criminals.

Consider how in this world, believers sometimes wish for exclusive spaces - gatherings where only Muslims are present, free from those who mock or hate them, free from inappropriate behavior or hostile glances. On that Day, Allah ﷻ Himself will screen everyone else out. The criminals will be told to separate themselves, to be distinguished and cut off from the blessed gathering of believers.

translation: Did I not enjoin upon you, O children of Adam, that you not worship Satan - [for] indeed, he is to you a clear enemy
commentary:
۞ أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ أَن لَّا تَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

"Didn't I take a promise from all of you, O children of Adam, that you would not worship Satan? Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy."

This ayah reveals another dimension of the covenant mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf (7:172), where Allah ﷻ asked all souls: "Am I not your Master?" and they responded: "Yes, we testify." Now we learn another part of that primordial promise - that we would not worship or follow Satan. Allah ﷻ emphasizes that there should be no doubt: Satan is an enemy who makes his enmity clear and obvious.

translation: And that you worship [only] Me? This is a straight path
commentary:
وَأَنِ ٱعْبُدُونِى ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ

"And that you should worship Me - this is the Straight Path." The command is direct and unambiguous: enslave yourselves to Me alone, worship Me exclusively. This worship of Allah ﷻ alone - this is what constitutes the صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ, the Straight Path that leads to success and Paradise.

translation: And he had already led astray from among you much of creation, so did you not use reason
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَضَلَّ مِنكُمْ جِبِلًّا كَثِيرًا ۖ أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْقِلُونَ

"And he has already led astray from among you great multitudes. Then did you not use reason?"

The word جِبِلًّا comes from the same root as جَبَل - mountain. It refers to an extremely large group, so massive that it brings to mind an endless mountain range. Allah ﷻ is saying that Satan has misguided huge multitudes upon multitudes of people - quantities as vast as ranges of mountains stretching across the earth.

The ayah ends with a stinging question: أَفَلَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْقِلُونَ - "Then did you not use reason?" Wasn't there even a little bit of intellect exercised? Was there never going to be a moment when you would use that brain that was given to you?

translation: This is the Hellfire which you were promised
commentary:
هَـٰذِهِۦ جَهَنَّمُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ

"This is Hellfire which you were promised." The demonstrative هَـٰذِهِۦ - "this" - makes it immediate and present. This is that very Hellfire that was warned about throughout your lives, that you were repeatedly promised would be the consequence of rejecting the truth.

translation: [Enter to] burn therein today for what you used to deny
commentary:
ٱصْلَوْهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ

"Enter it today for what you used to deny." The command اصْلَوْهَا means to throw yourselves into it, to be thrown into it. Today, on account of the ingratitude and disbelief that characterized your worldly life, you will enter this Fire.

translation: That Day, We will seal over their mouths, and their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will testify about what they used to earn
commentary:
ٱلْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلَىٰٓ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنَآ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ

"Today We will seal their mouths, and their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will testify about what they used to earn."

Your mouths have done enough talking - no more words from your mouths. Now your hands will do the talking. The hands will testify to every action: "He took me there. He used me to sign those papers. He made me reach for that." The feet will bear witness: "He walked me to that club. He took me to her house. He made me go to that theater. He walked me into that bank."

Every single thing is being testified to by the very limbs that committed those actions. The hands and feet are testifying بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ - regarding everything that they earned, every deed they accumulated in the worldly life.

translation: And if We willed, We could have obliterated their eyes, and they would race to [find] the path, and how could they see
commentary:
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَطَمَسْنَا عَلَىٰٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ فَٱسْتَبَقُوا۟ ٱلصِّرَٰطَ فَأَنَّىٰ يُبْصِرُونَ

"And if We willed, We could have obliterated their eyes, then they would race to the path, but how could they see?"

The word طَمَسْنَا comes from طَمْس, which means to rub something continuously until no sign of it remains - like buffing or grinding a surface until it becomes completely smooth. The image is graphic: if you take a rock and chisel it for days, it becomes smooth. Modern machines can grind wood planks or buffer surfaces until they're perfectly smooth. This is tams - to obliterate by constant rubbing until nothing is left.

Allah ﷻ is saying: had We wanted, We would have obliterated their eyes by rubbing them until there's nothing left - no eyes, just smoothed-out surfaces. Then they would try to race to the path, but how would they see? They would be utterly helpless.

translation: And if We willed, We could have deformed them, [paralyzing them] in their places so they would not be able to proceed, nor could they return
commentary:
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَمَسَخْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِهِمْ فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مُضِيًّا وَلَا يَرْجِعُونَ
