"And if We willed, We could have deformed them in their places, so they would not be able to proceed, nor could they return."

The word مَسَخْنَا means to deform or disfigure someone's face, to make it twisted or mangled. Allah ﷻ could freeze them in their tracks with deformed faces, unable to move forward and unable to go back to what they once were. They would be trapped, unable to return to their original state.

translation: And he to whom We grant long life We reverse in creation; so will they not understand
commentary:
وَمَن نُّعَمِّرْهُ نُنَكِّسْهُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ ۖ أَفَلَا يَعْقِلُونَ

"And he to whom We grant long life - We reverse him in creation. Then will they not use reason?"

How does this connect to the previous ayah about not being able to return? Allah ﷻ provides a glimpse of this reality in human life itself. Whoever We give old age, We reverse them in creation - We wind them down, roll them back. The word نُنَكِّسْهُ means to put something down, to deteriorate it, to roll it back.

Consider the remarkable parallels: when we're very young, we tire easily, can't walk long distances, need to sit down frequently. We can't feed ourselves - we must be fed. We can't clean ourselves - we must be cleaned. Our vision is blurry. We're emotionally volatile, crying and upset easily.

What happens in old age? The same dependencies return. We tire easily again. We can't feed ourselves. We can't clean ourselves. Vision becomes blurry. We become cranky and emotionally sensitive. An elderly person can say the harshest things, but you can't respond because they're at an age where you must simply let it go, just as you would with a child.

The scholar Ibn Abbas (RA) offered a brilliant insight: in youth, the spine is upright. But as people age, their backs begin to bend until they're constantly looking downward. Allah ﷻ is reminding them: you came from earth, and now you keep looking at earth because that's where you're headed again.

translation: And We did not give Prophet Muhammad, knowledge of poetry, nor is it befitting for him. It is not but a message and a clear Qur'an
commentary:
وَمَا عَلَّمْنَـٰهُ ٱلشِّعْرَ وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ وَقُرْءَانٌ مُّبِينٌ

"And We did not teach him poetry, nor is it befitting for him. It is not but a reminder and a clear Quran."

After discussing the reality of human creation and decline, Allah ﷻ returns to address the accusation that the Quran is merely poetry. This addresses not just the Quraysh's claim but a deeper misunderstanding: that this message is mere entertainment.

When Allah ﷻ says "We didn't teach him poetry," He's also saying we didn't teach him entertainment. The religion is not entertainment. Speeches are not entertainment. Khutbahs are not entertainment. The recitation of the Quran is not just entertainment. If your entire relationship with the Quran amounts to enjoying its beautiful presentation - which is wonderful in itself - but that's all it is, then you've missed the point.

The Quran is undeniably beautiful. There's no greater joy than learning it, memorizing it, reciting it and listening to its recitation. But that beauty is a bonus, an added benefit. Allah ﷻ could have guided us in a way that wasn't this beautiful. He didn't have to make it so enjoyable to recite. But when people treat this beauty as the primary purpose at the expense of the message's seriousness, they've fundamentally misunderstood.

Consider the absurdity: someone beautifully recites ayahs about people being thrown into Hellfire, and the audience who doesn't understand Arabic sits there saying "MashaAllah, so beautiful!" It's not beautiful - it's terrifying. Or someone recites ayahs about inheritance laws and people behind him are crying artificially. Why are you crying about inheritance distribution when you don't even know what's being recited?

This is supposed to move us genuinely, not like entertainment moves people who cry over fictional Disney characters. This is about our own reality, our own fate.

Poetry doesn't fit the Prophet ﷺ. Interestingly, he loved poetry and appreciated it deeply. He would say, "Indeed, in eloquent speech there is magic." But he couldn't compose or even accurately remember it. No matter how hard he tried, if asked to repeat a piece of poetry, he would inadvertently change it. Poetry requires remembering not just words but the meter, the rhythm, the precise syllabic breaks.

Think about this: the Prophet ﷺ memorized the entire Quran without it being written, while multiple surahs were still being revealed incompletely, knowing exactly which ayah belonged where - yet he couldn't remember a simple piece of poetry. This is the man who could retain divine revelation perfectly, but when it came to poetry, Allah ﷻ simply didn't give him that ability.

There's a beautiful incident where the Prophet ﷺ attempted to recite poetry that mentioned two tribes - let's call them Tribe A and Tribe B. He recited it with B and A reversed. A companion gently corrected him: "O Messenger of Allah, I think it's A and B, not B and A." The Prophet ﷺ simply responded: "What difference does it make? It's the same thing."

The companion's iman was renewed at that moment. "You really are the messenger of Allah," he said. The Prophet ﷺ's inability to master this worldly art was itself proof of his prophethood. Allah ﷻ deliberately didn't give this most intelligent human being the ability to excel at poetry, despite his appreciation for it, so no one could claim the Quran was the product of poetic talent.

وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُۥٓ - "nor is it befitting for him." It doesn't fit him, because this message is far more serious than entertainment or poetic exercise. إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ - "It is nothing but a reminder" - وَقُرْءَانٌ مُّبِينٌ - "and a clear Quran."

translation: To warn whoever is alive and justify the word against the disbelievers
commentary:
لِّيُنذِرَ مَن كَانَ حَيًّا وَيَحِقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

"To warn whoever is alive and that the word may come true against the disbelievers."

This connects beautifully to the previous surah's message: you cannot make anyone listen who is in the grave. The Quran came to warn مَن كَانَ حَيًّا - whoever is still alive. But who does this mean? Those who are spiritually alive.

There are people whose hearts are dead - those mentioned at the beginning of this surah, whose faculties have been sealed. They're spiritually deceased. The Quran cannot benefit them. But whoever still has spiritual life, whoever's heart still beats with the possibility of faith - those are the ones who can be warned.

وَيَحِقَّ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ - "And so the verdict could become manifest and deserving upon those who disbelieved." For those who reject, the word of judgment becomes established and worthy to be executed against them.

translation: Do they not see that We have created for them from what Our hands have made, grazing livestock, and [then] they are their owners
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَا لَهُم مِّمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا فَهُمْ لَهَا مَـٰلِكُونَ

"Do they not see that We have created for them from what Our hands have made, livestock, and they are their owners?"

Didn't they observe? Didn't they see? Allah ﷻ shifts to discussing His blessings and signs. We created for them, from the work of Our own hands, various types of livestock - cattle, sheep, goats, camels. فَهُمْ لَهَا مَـٰلِكُونَ - and they own them.

But consider: did you create those four legs? Did you design the udders that produce milk? Did you manufacture the milk itself? You own them, but you didn't make them.

translation: And We have tamed them for them, so some of them they ride, and some of them they eat
commentary:
وَذَلَّلْنَـٰهَا لَهُمْ فَمِنْهَا رَكُوبُهُمْ وَمِنْهَا يَأْكُلُونَ

"And We have tamed them for them, so some of them they ride, and some of them they eat."

The word ذَلَّلْنَا means We subdued them, We humiliated them, We domesticated them. Had Allah ﷻ wanted, the cow could have been one of the most vicious predators in nature, hunting human beings. He subdued it, put it to rest for our benefit.

Think about it: this is a massive creature. Allah ﷻ could have given it slightly stronger teeth, somewhat more powerful legs, and we would be in serious trouble. But He didn't. فَمِنْهَا رَكُوبُهُمْ - from them is what they ride - وَمِنْهَا يَأْكُلُونَ - and from them is what they consume. They get the milk while the animal lives, and when the milk runs out, they consume the meat.

translation: And for them therein are [other] benefits and drinks, so will they not be grateful
commentary:
وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ وَمَشَارِبُ ۖ أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ

"And for them therein are other benefits and drinks. Then will they not be grateful?"

Beyond riding and eating, there are مَنَـٰفِعُ - other benefits from these animals. Their hides, their wool, their bones. In our time, not a single part of these animals goes to waste - everything is utilized. وَمَشَارِبُ - and various forms of drink from them as well.

أَفَلَا يَشْكُرُونَ - Then why don't they show gratitude? With all these blessings so obvious, why isn't there thanksgiving?

translation: But they have taken besides Allah [false] deities that perhaps they would be helped
commentary:
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لَّعَلَّهُمْ يُنصَرُونَ

"But they have taken besides Allah other gods that perhaps they would be helped."

Despite all these clear signs of Allah's provision and power, they've taken other than Allah as objects of worship - gods, idols, entities they turn to for help. لَّعَلَّهُمْ يُنصَرُونَ - hoping that perhaps they might be aided by them.

translation: They are not able to help them, and they [themselves] are for them soldiers in attendance
commentary:
لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُندٌ مُّحْضَرُونَ

"They are not able to help them, and they are for them soldiers in attendance."

Those false gods لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَهُمْ - cannot help their worshipers at all. وَهُمْ لَهُمْ جُندٌ مُّحْضَرُونَ - rather, they will be brought as an army against them. The idols themselves will be made to stand as witnesses against their worshipers on the Day of Judgment. The armies of angels, the false gods they worshiped - all will testify against them, saying, "We never wanted to be worshiped. We're innocent of what they attributed to us."

translation: So let not their speech grieve you. Indeed, We know what they conceal and what they declare
commentary:
فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ

"So let not their speech grieve you. Indeed, We know what they conceal and what they declare."

After describing the reality of false worship, Allah ﷻ comforts His Messenger ﷺ: فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ - don't let their words grieve you, don't let their speech worry you. Their mockery, their rejection, their arguments - don't let these things burden your heart.

إِنَّا نَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ - We already know the things they keep secret and the things they show openly. Nothing is hidden from Us. We know their plots, their schemes, their private counsels and their public declarations.

translation: Does man not consider that We created him from a [mere] sperm-drop - then at once he is a clear adversary
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ

"Does man not consider that We created him from a drop? Then suddenly he is a clear adversary."

Hasn't the human being observed his own origin? أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ - that We created him from a نُّطْفَة - a drop of fluid, something insignificant and impure. فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ - then suddenly, out of nowhere, he becomes someone who argues openly, clearly, constantly.

The parallel is striking: imagine a child you once carried, fed, cleaned and cared for in complete helplessness. Years later, that same child stands before you raising his voice in argument. You might say, "There was a day I held you in my arms and changed your diapers. I carried you in my belly. Look how far you've come that you can argue with me like this now."

This is what Allah ﷻ is saying to the human being. Look at what you were made from - this insignificant drop - and now you have the audacity to argue? The word خَصِيمٌ indicates someone constantly engaged in disputation, always looking for arguments and debates. مُّبِينٌ (mubin) means he thinks his arguments are so clear, so obvious, so compelling.

Allah ﷻ is speaking about the arrogant type of person who's always seeking debate, always challenging, despite having been created from the humblest origin.

translation: And he presents for Us an example and forgets his [own] creation. He says, "Who will give life to bones while they are disintegrated
commentary:
وَضَرَبَ لَنَا مَثَلًا وَنَسِىَ خَلْقَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ مَن يُحْىِ ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ وَهِىَ رَمِيمٌ

"And he presents for Us an example and forgets his own creation. He says, 'Who will give life to bones while they are disintegrated?'"

وَضَرَبَ لَنَا مَثَلًا - He starts presenting examples for Us, constructing hypotheses and arguments. He engages in philosophical debates about resurrection. وَنَسِىَ خَلْقَهُۥ - The only thing missing from his entire intellectual exercise is the memory of his own creation. He forgot how he himself was made.

قَالَ مَن يُحْىِ ٱلْعِظَـٰمَ وَهِىَ رَمِيمٌ - He asks, "Who will give life to bones when they have disintegrated?" He finds it impossible to imagine that decayed bones could be brought back to life, while completely forgetting that he himself was created from something far less substantial than bones - from a mere drop of fluid.

The irony is complete: the one who was made from nothing substantial questions whether bones can be reassembled and given life again. This is the ultimate form of forgetfulness and ingratitude.

translation: Say, "He will give them life who produced them the first time; and He is, of all creation, Knowing
commentary:
قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَهَآ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ: "Tell him: He's the One who initiated it, who raised it the first time around. He will bring it back again." This ayah presents a fundamental logical principle: if something is accomplished the first time, doing it a second time should be easier, not harder. 

The One who created you from nothing—وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ (and He is knowledgeable of all forms of creation)—possesses complete knowledge of every aspect of creation. If you recognize that Allah ﷻ created you in the first place, then believing in the resurrection becomes the easier step, not the harder one. Believing in life after death is actually a smaller leap of faith than believing in the original creation from nothing.

translation: [It is] He who made for you from the green tree, fire, and then from it you ignite
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ ٱلْأَخْضَرِ نَارًا فَإِذَآ أَنتُم مِّنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ

Allah ﷻ presents another proof from nature: "The One who made fire for you out of the green tree." Consider the miracle embedded in this reality. A green tree is full of water—greenery itself depends on water, grows from water, stores water within its structure. Yet from that same source that collects and drains water, Allah ﷻ creates fire. 

فَإِذَآ أَنتُم مِّنْهُ تُوقِدُونَ (then out of nowhere, you start creating and building fires from wood). This is a phenomenon we witness regularly but rarely stop to marvel at: how does the moist, water-filled tree become the source of fire? The One who can bring opposites together—fire from water-bearing wood—can certainly bring life from decayed bones.

translation: Is not He who created the heavens and the earth Able to create the likes of them? Yes, [it is so]; and He is the Knowing Creator
commentary:
أَوَلَيْسَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَهُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

"Isn't the One who created the heavens and the earth capable of creating the likes of them?" The phrase مِثْلَهُم (the likes of them) carries two profound implications. 

First, it addresses human arrogance: Do you think you are some special kind of creature that's too difficult to reproduce? The One who created the vast expanse of the heavens and earth—with all their complexity, their systems, their intricate balance—is certainly capable of recreating something as comparatively small as a human being.

Second, this phrase hints at an important reality about resurrection: when Allah ﷻ recreates us, it won't be exactly the same body we have now. It will be a new version of us—a body designed for the eternal realm. Some bodies will be made for Jannah, others for the Fire. Just as the fruits of Jannah appear similar to the fruits of this world but are fundamentally different in their nature and perfection, so too will our resurrected forms be similar to our current bodies but adapted for eternal existence.

بَلَىٰ وَهُوَ ٱلْخَلَّـٰقُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (Rather, He is Al-Khallaq, Al-Aleem—the Continuous Creator, the All-Knowing). The name Al-Khallaq doesn't simply mean "Creator" but implies one who creates over and over and over again. Even within this life, continuous creation is happening. Modern biology has revealed what the Quran already indicated: within approximately a decade, every cell in our body has been replaced. Old cells die and new cells take their place. We are, in a biological sense, continuously being recreated. A form of death and rebirth is already occurring within us. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere, سَنُرِيهِمْ آيَاتِنَا فِي الْآفَاقِ وَفِي أَنفُسِهِمْ (We will show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves). The study of the human being itself reveals truths about Allah's ﷻ power.

translation: His command is only when He intends a thing that He says to it, "Be," and it is
commentary:
إِنَّمَآ أَمْرُهُۥٓ إِذَآ أَرَادَ شَيْـًٔا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

"His command, whenever He intends something, is only to say to it 'Be,' and it becomes." This ayah contains a fascinating combination of words. Notice إِذَآ أَرَادَ شَيْـًٔا (when He intends a thing). The word شَيْء typically means "thing," but its linguistic root reveals something deeper. It comes from شَاءَ, meaning "will." When you have a strong will—so strong that it materializes into reality—that's when something becomes a shay', a "thing." 

For us, there's a journey from intention to reality. We have weak intentions that never materialize and strong intentions that eventually become things. But for Allah ﷻ, every intention is already so powerful that it is immediately a shay'. There is no such thing as a weak intention from Allah ﷻ. Whatever He intends instantly has the strength of reality.

This is why the process is so immediate: أَن يَقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (He says to it "Be," and it becomes). There is no delay, no process, no stages. The command and the existence are virtually simultaneous.

translation: So exalted is He in whose hand is the realm of all things, and to Him you will be returned
commentary:
فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِۦ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

The surah concludes with a powerful declaration: فَسُبْحَـٰنَ (so how perfect is) the One in whose hands is مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (the ultimate dominion, the complete kingdom of all things). The word مَلَكُوت intensifies the concept of ملك (kingdom). It emphasizes the absolute, unshakeable, eternal nature of His authority over everything.

وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (and to Him alone you are all going to be returned). After all these arguments, after all these proofs of Allah's ﷻ power to resurrect, the surah ends with this certainty: we will all return to Him. The question was never whether resurrection would happen. The only question was whether the disbeliever would recognize the obvious truth before it was too late.

This is the conclusion of Surah Ya-Sin—a surah that began with affirming the Messenger ﷺ, moved through the stories of nations that rejected their messengers, reminded us of Allah's ﷻ signs in creation, and ended with the undeniable reality of resurrection and return.
